summary: Judy Faulkner reflects on the origins of Epic's interoperability efforts, noting that early resistance from health systems over liability and data quality was overcome by a 'lucky break.' She emphasizes her foundational belief that patient data must be shareable across vendors to save lives, provided the patient grants permission.

principles.statement: Patient data must be universally shareable across vendors, provided the patient grants permission.
principles.rationale: Having access to medical records across state lines and different health systems can save patients' lives in emergency situations.
principles.source_quote: Epic’s goal had always been that regardless of vendor, every patient’s data should be able to be shared, with the patient’s permission, wherever the patient went.

signature_quotes.text: Epic’s goal had always been that regardless of vendor, every patient’s data should be able to be shared, with the patient’s permission, wherever the patient went.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the foundational philosophy behind Epic's push for healthcare interoperability.

signature_quotes.text: The CEO did not ask what the software did. He explained to the group of CEOs [at an Epic meeting] that if he had known what it was for, he would not have signed it! Lucky break, finally, for Interoperability!
signature_quotes.context: Recounting how the first health system adopted Epic's Care Everywhere platform despite widespread industry reluctance.

anti_patterns.description: Allowing liability and data quality concerns to prevent the sharing of patient medical records.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It blocks interoperability, which ultimately prevents doctors from accessing critical medical history that could save a patient's life in an emergency.
